title: Mornings with Graham & Emmy
teaser: New dashboard tracks every local fight; Williamston residents push back hard today.

lines.text: Good morning, and welcome to Mornings with Graham & Emmy — the Michigan Data Wire, your podcast on Michigan's data center buildout. I'm Graham.
And I'm Emmy.
It's Thursday, July 23, and a brand-new statewide map just dropped showing every active data center fight in Michigan right now.
That sounds like the perfect way to kick off our conversation today.
Exactly. The MLive dashboard lets anyone click on a township or county and see proposals, upcoming hearings, moratoria, megawatts demanded, and even potential tax deals in real time.
So residents can finally compare what's happening near them to battles happening elsewhere?
That's the idea. It surfaces how projects stack up across the state instead of each community fighting in the dark.
Why does that matter so much right now?
Because data centers tied to AI are driving massive new power demand, and local governments are scrambling to write rules before the next application lands.
We've seen this pattern before with other big infrastructure projects, right?
Yes, but the speed and scale here are different. A single hyperscale campus can pull hundreds of megawatts, which is like adding a small city overnight.
And that hits electric bills, water supplies, and roads all at once.
Exactly. Utilities have to build new lines or substations, and those costs eventually show up on ratepayer bills.
What about the water angle? Some places are talking about wells drying up or big withdrawals.
Cooling systems for these facilities can use millions of gallons a day in some designs. Rural townships with private wells are especially worried.
And farmland? A lot of these proposals are on ag land.
Right. Once rezoned, that ground is gone for farming, and towns have to weigh the tax revenue against the cost of new services and infrastructure.
So the map is really showing a patchwork of local decisions that add up to a statewide issue.
It is. Today we saw Williamston Township postpone new zoning rules, leaving residents without clear limits on where these facilities can go.
And they had a packed hearing last night where hundreds showed up asking about traffic, noise, and rates.
No vote was taken, so the uncertainty stays high for homes and farms right next door.
Meanwhile other places are moving faster. Pittsfield Township near Ann Arbor is already drafting performance standards ahead of a potential big project.
They're trying to get guardrails in place before any formal application arrives.
That makes sense. What happens if the rules end up too loose or too strict?
Residents thirty minutes away could still feel the impact on roads, water, and electric rates either way.
And then there's the legal side. Monroe Township is facing a three-hundred-million-dollar lawsuit after banning data centers.
A developer claims the ban killed a major project, and residents could end up paying higher taxes or legal costs if the township loses.
That's a big financial risk for a small community.
It shows how these zoning fights are now attracting serious litigation in southeast Michigan.
Up north, Lovells Township just enacted a year-long ban to buy time for permanent rules.
Crawford County officials gave the small township breathing room before any large projects arrive.
Chelsea did something similar, freezing data centers and big rental projects for twelve months while they study infrastructure limits.
Neighbors get a pause on sudden spikes in power demand and traffic.
And in Augusta Township, voters decide on August fourth whether to rezone three hundred acres for a possible campus.
Approval would open the door to hundreds of megawatts on Consumers Energy lines and possible well withdrawals.
A no vote would keep the land in agriculture or low-density use.
Then there's the court ruling that overturned a local veto of a massive AI campus, reopening a sixteen-billion-dollar project.
So the fight moved from the township board to the courtroom.
Neighbors are back to asking who pays for new power lines and whether wells and roads can handle it.
And in West Michigan, homeowners are suing a hyperscale facility over constant noise.
The lawsuit puts operators on notice that residents won't quietly accept the side effects.
So the map really captures how these issues are playing out everywhere at once.
It does. And it lets people see the common threads: power demand, water use, noise, taxes, and who decides.
What do you think happens next as more townships see this dashboard?
Probably more moratoria while officials study, more public hearings, and more lawsuits testing the limits of local control.
Will the map itself change how residents organize?
It could. Seeing similar fights in neighboring counties might encourage shared strategies or joint pressure on utilities.
And the utilities themselves? How are they responding to all these local pauses?
They're watching closely because delayed projects still need the same grid upgrades eventually.
So the uncertainty doesn't go away; it just shifts.
Right. The map makes that visible in one place for the first time.
I wonder how many townships are currently paused or writing rules.
The dashboard should start answering that as more data gets added.
For now it already shows the scale of the conversation happening across Michigan.
And that brings us to our quick hits on the other stories from the past day.
Let's start with Monroe Township's lawsuit. A developer is suing for three hundred million dollars after the ban killed a project.
Residents could face higher taxes or legal costs if the township loses, and it highlights how big-money litigation is entering these fights.
Next, Lovells Township enacted a year-long moratorium to write permanent rules.
Crawford County gave the small northern township time before large projects arrive, addressing worries over noise, water, and tax breaks.
Chelsea city council also voted a full-year freeze on new data centers and large rentals.
That pause lets neighbors avoid sudden spikes in power demand and traffic while the city studies its limits.
In Augusta Township, voters decide August fourth on rezoning three hundred acres.
Approval would bring hundreds of megawatts of demand and possible well withdrawals; a no vote keeps the land agricultural.
A court overturned one township's veto of a massive AI campus, reopening a sixteen-billion-dollar project.
The fight now shifts to the courtroom over power lines, wells, and roads.
And West Michigan homeowners sued a hyperscale facility over constant noise.
The operator is on notice that neighbors expect changes or compensation.
That's a solid round of updates. Now let's look at what's on deck for listeners who want to get involved.
Today the Oxford Township Planning Commission is discussing a proposed draft data center ordinance.
It's listed as a starting point for public input with no action or specific projects under review.
The Charter Township of Oxford Planning Commission has the same item on its agenda.
So residents can show up and weigh in on the draft language before anything gets locked in.
Full sources and the live map at the Michigan Data Center Tracker.
We're back tomorrow.

episode_title: Live Map Reveals Michigan's Data Center Battles as Townships Weigh Power, Water and Growth
